data.title: Is It Time To Revisit Commvault Systems (CVLT) After Its 50% Share Price Slide
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Commvault Systems at around US$79.80 is starting to look attractive, the answer depends on how you think about what "fair value" really means for this stock.
  • The share price has seen a 1.3% decline over the last week, a 9.8% decline over the last month, a 35.7% decline year to date, and a 50.8% decline over the last year, which can change how the market views both its potential and its risks.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Commvault Systems as a data protection and backup software provider that sits in a competitive software space, so shifts in sentiment around software spending and data management needs have been in focus. This context helps explain why a stock that has 45.1% returns over three years and 25.6% over five years can still experience sharp pullbacks.
  • Simply Wall St currently gives Commvault Systems a valuation score of 1 out of 6, so the key question for you is how that compares with other ways of judging value today and whether a more complete view at the end of this article changes how you see the stock.

Commvault Systems scores just 1/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Commvault Systems Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what those future dollars are worth in current terms.

For Commvault Systems, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $183.1 million. Analysts and model estimates project annual free cash flow in the range of about $215.0 million in 2026, rising through a set of forecasts and extrapolations up to $269.97 million by 2035, with $277.1 million indicated for 2029. Simply Wall St uses analyst inputs for the earlier years and then extrapolates beyond that.

When these projected cash flows are discounted back, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $83.31 per share. Compared with the recent share price around US$79.80, this implies the stock is about 4.2% undervalued, which sits in a fairly modest range.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Commvault Systems is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Commvault Systems.

Approach 2: Commvault Systems Price vs Earnings

For a profitable software company, the P/E ratio is a practical way to think about value because it links what you pay directly to the earnings the business is already generating. Investors usually accept a higher P/E when they expect stronger earnings growth or see lower risk, and look for a lower P/E when growth expectations are more modest or risks feel higher.

Commvault Systems currently trades on a P/E of 40.3x, which is above both the broader Software industry average of 29.0x and the peer average of 23.9x. On the surface, that suggests the market is paying a richer price for each dollar of Commvault’s earnings compared with many software names.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio framework goes a step further by estimating what a more tailored P/E might look like once factors such as Commvault’s earnings growth profile, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks are taken into account. On this basis, Commvault’s Fair Ratio is 33.0x. Compared with the current 40.3x, this points to the shares trading above that Fair Ratio based view of value.

Result: OVERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Commvault Systems Narrative

Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. On Simply Wall St that means using Narratives, where you choose a clear story for Commvault Systems, link it to specific forecasts for revenue, earnings and margins, and then see the Fair Value that follows so you can compare it with the current share price.

A Narrative is your view of what is really driving Commvault Systems, captured as a simple storyline plus numbers. For example, one investor might lean toward a more cautious view that lines up with a Fair Value around US$140.33, while another might lean toward a more optimistic view closer to US$185.00. Both are valid as long as the story and forecast are consistent.

On the Simply Wall St Community page, used by millions of investors, Narratives are updated automatically when new data, news or earnings are added. data.title: IBM Confluent Deal Aims To Anchor Real Time AI Data Strategy
data.description:
• IBM (NYSE:IBM) has completed its US$11b acquisition of Confluent, a provider of real-time data streaming technology.
  • The deal is intended to support a unified platform for AI and automation that works with continuously flowing, governed data.
  • IBM plans to apply Confluent’s capabilities across on premises and hybrid cloud environments as part of its AI and data strategy.

For you as an investor, this move ties directly into IBM’s focus on AI and hybrid cloud services rather than just traditional hardware and software. By bringing Confluent into the fold, IBM is concentrating on the data plumbing that large enterprises rely on to run AI models on operational information in real time.

Looking ahead, the key question is how effectively IBM can integrate Confluent’s streaming platform into its existing AI, automation and data products. The outcome will likely be reflected in how many large clients choose IBM as a core provider for their AI data infrastructure and in how central this real-time platform becomes in long term contracts.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for International Business Machines by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on International Business Machines.

4 things going right for International Business Machines that this headline doesn't cover.

The Confluent deal sits at the core of how IBM wants you to think about its AI and hybrid-cloud offerings. Confluent brings real-time data streaming that can feed IBM’s watsonx platform, automation tools, and consulting projects with constantly updated, governed data rather than static batches. That is relevant when you line it up with IBM’s expanded work with NVIDIA, its quantum-centric architecture, and its Storage Scale systems, all of which are aimed at heavy data and AI workloads. Together, they sketch a clearer picture of IBM trying to be the data and infrastructure backbone for enterprises running AI agents across multiple environments, including on-premises setups where competitors such as Microsoft, Amazon, and Google are also very active.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The International Business Machines Narrative

  • The acquisition directly supports the existing narrative that IBM wants hybrid cloud and AI to drive higher quality earnings, by adding a real-time data layer that can make software such as watsonx and Red Hat OpenShift more useful to large clients.
  • If integration of Confluent proves complex or slower than expected, it could challenge the narrative that IBM can steadily raise margins while absorbing acquisitions and maintaining consistent growth in Software and Consulting.
  • The narrative focuses heavily on earnings targets and broader hybrid-cloud execution, while this specific shift toward a unified real-time data platform, and how it links into deals like the NVIDIA collaboration, is only partially reflected.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for International Business Machines to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ IBM carries a high level of debt, so large cash deals such as the US$11b Confluent acquisition can add pressure to the balance sheet if expected cash flows and synergies take time to materialize.
  • ⚠️ IBM is moving into areas where Microsoft, Amazon, and Google already offer AI and data platforms, so there is a risk that customer budgets favor rival ecosystems even as IBM scales up Confluent and watsonx.
  • 🎁 Analysts highlight 4 key rewards for IBM, including a view that the shares trade below some fair-value estimates and that earnings have grown strongly over the past year, which provides a base for funding AI and data investments.
  • 🎁 Confluent’s real-time streaming, when combined with IBM’s Storage Scale, quantum-centric architecture, and NVIDIA collaboration, gives IBM a more complete story for clients that want end-to-end AI data infrastructure across on-premises and hybrid-cloud setups.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, you may want to watch for concrete signs that IBM is selling Confluent-powered, AI-ready data platforms into its largest accounts, for example through bundled software and consulting deals or references to Confluent in multi-year contracts. Updates on how often IBM mentions Confluent alongside watsonx, Red Hat, and NVIDIA in client case studies can help you judge whether this is becoming a core part of the portfolio rather than a stand-alone asset. It is also useful to monitor how debt levels and funding needs evolve, including any further shelf registrations, as IBM continues to invest in AI, quantum, and security offerings.

data.title: Orla Mining Q4 Net Margin At 7% Tests Bullish High Growth Narratives
data.description:
Orla Mining (TSX:OLA) closed out FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of US$378.5 million and basic EPS of US$0.23, alongside trailing twelve month revenue of US$1.1 billion and EPS of US$0.33 that reflect a very large year over year earnings lift and earnings growth of 1,107.1%. Over the past year, the company has seen revenue move from US$343.9 million in the prior trailing period to US$1.1 billion and net income shift from US$89.0 million to US$106.9 million on a trailing basis. This sets the stage for investors to focus on how the current 7% net margin shapes the story from here.

See our full analysis for Orla Mining.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to set these results against the most widely held narratives around Orla Mining to see which stories line up with the data and which ones start to look out of sync.

See what the community is saying about Orla Mining

Advertisement

Revenue climbs toward US$1.1b run rate

  • On a trailing basis, Orla Mining generated about US$1.1b of revenue, compared with US$343.9 million in the prior trailing period, with quarterly revenue in FY 2025 moving from US$140.7 million in Q1 to US$378.5 million in Q4.
  • Bulls often point to this revenue ramp, together with new projects, as evidence that production growth can support their growth story, yet:
    • Trailing twelve month net income of US$106.9 million on US$1.1b of revenue lines up with a 7% margin, which is still relatively slim for a business priced on high growth expectations.
    • Forecasts in the dataset call for revenue to grow about 28% per year, so investors who agree with the bullish view may want to see whether this trailing revenue trend feels consistent with that pace.
Bulls argue that the current revenue base is only the starting point for Orla Mining. It can be useful to see how that view is built out in more detail in the 🐂 Orla Mining Bull Case.

Margins at 7% while growth projects ramp

  • Net profit margin sits at 7% on the latest trailing twelve month figures, up from 1.4% in the prior trailing period, with quarterly net income for FY 2025 ranging from a loss of US$69.8 million in Q1 to a profit of US$79.2 million in Q4.
  • Bears highlight that planned growth needs to overcome cost and execution pressures, and the current margin profile gives them a few talking points:
    • The shift from a Q1 loss to positive net income later in the year coincides with higher quarterly revenue, so a lot of the current 7% margin is tied to scaling production rather than visibly lower cost intensity.
    • Concerns around items like pit wall issues at Camino Rojo and the capital demands of South Railroad tie back to this margin, because any cost creep would flow directly through a relatively thin net income buffer.
Skeptics often focus on whether a 7% margin is enough to absorb project risk. It can be helpful to see how that cautious view is laid out in the 🐻 Orla Mining Bear Case.

High-growth earnings meet premium 85x P/E

  • Trailing twelve month earnings growth is described as very large at 1,107.1%, while the stock trades on an 85x P/E against peer and industry averages of 15.8x and 17.4x, and a DCF fair value of CA$73.71 compared with a current share price of CA$19.29.
  • The consensus narrative flags a tension between rapid earnings growth and valuation, and the numbers capture that contrast clearly:
    • Analysts in the dataset refer to forecast earnings growth of about 81.9% per year, which goes a long way toward explaining why the market is willing to assign a P/E that is several times higher than peers.
    • At the same time, the gap between the CA$19.29 share price, the 32.25 analyst price target cap and the higher DCF fair value highlights how different valuation methods can point to very different outcomes that investors need to weigh for themselves.

After weighing the bullish and cautious angles, the real question is what you make of the story. Take a moment to test the numbers, pressure check the assumptions, and see what stands out in the 4 key rewards.

data.description:

  • The stock has pulled back recently, with an approximate 2.8% decline over the last week and 12.5% over the last month, even though the 1 year return sits at 36.2% and the 3 year and 5 year figures are very large.
  • That mix of shorter term weakness and stronger longer term performance has kept Lloyds in the spotlight, with investors reassessing whether recent price levels reflect changing expectations or simply normal volatility. Evergreen interest in UK banks and ongoing attention on large domestically focused lenders provide context for why Lloyds continues to draw valuation questions.
  • Lloyds currently scores 2 out of 6 on Simply Wall St's value checks, as shown by its valuation score. The rest of this article will walk through what that score actually means using several valuation approaches before closing with a way to put those numbers into a broader investing framework.

Lloyds Banking Group scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Lloyds Banking Group Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company is expected to earn on its equity above the return shareholders require, then converts those extra profits into an estimate of fair value per share.

For Lloyds, the starting point is a Book Value of £0.71 per share and a Stable Book Value estimate of £0.82 per share, based on forecasts from 8 analysts. On that equity base, Stable EPS is £0.12 per share, sourced from weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 12 analysts.

The Cost of Equity is £0.07 per share, so the model estimates an Excess Return of £0.05 per share. That is underpinned by an Average Return on Equity of 14.69%, which is higher than the required return implied by the cost of equity. The model converts these excess earnings into an intrinsic value of about £1.83 per share.

Compared with the current share price around £0.92, this implies the stock is about 50.0% undervalued on this measure.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests Lloyds Banking Group is undervalued by 50.0%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 10 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Lloyds Banking Group.

Approach 2: Lloyds Banking Group Price vs Earnings

For a profitable bank like Lloyds, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for how much you are paying for each pound of earnings. This makes it a common way to compare valuation across banks that all report relatively stable, recurring profits.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk profile. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk often align with higher P/E levels, while slower growth or higher risk tend to align with lower P/E levels.

Lloyds currently trades on a P/E of 12.80x. That sits above the Banks industry average of 10.71x and above the peer group average of 9.53x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Lloyds is 10.37x, which is a proprietary estimate of what the P/E might be given factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, risk profile, market cap and its position within the banking industry.

Because the Fair Ratio adjusts for these company specific drivers, it can be more informative than a simple peer or industry comparison. With the current P/E of 12.80x above the Fair Ratio of 10.37x, the shares screen as more expensive on this metric.

Result: OVERVALUED

A Narrative links three pieces together: your view of the business, a set of financial forecasts built from that view, and a fair value that falls out of those forecasts, so you can see in one place what would need to be true for your opinion on Lloyds to make sense.

On Simply Wall St, Narratives sit inside the Community page and are designed to be easy to use. Approach 1: Mondelez International Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects the cash a business is expected to generate in the future and then discounts those cash flows back to today to estimate what the company might be worth right now.

For Mondelez International, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. It is based on $3.20b in last twelve months free cash flow and a series of projected free cash flows out to 2035. Analyst estimates run through 2028, with Simply Wall St extrapolating further projections after that. For example, free cash flow is projected at $4.68b in 2028, with discounted values provided each year through 2035.

When all of those discounted cash flows are added together, the resulting estimated intrinsic value is $107.26 per share. Compared with the recent share price of about $56.49, the model implies a 47.3% discount to this intrinsic value. This suggests that the shares are trading at a lower level than this DCF estimate.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Mondelez International Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Mondelez International, the P/E ratio is a useful way to link what you pay per share to the earnings that support that share price. Investors usually accept a higher P/E when they expect stronger growth or see lower risk, and a lower P/E when growth expectations are more modest or risks feel higher.

Mondelez International currently trades on a P/E of 29.54x. That sits above the Food industry average P/E of 20.30x, yet below the peer group average of 42.39x. On its own, that mixed picture can be hard to interpret, because neither simple industry nor peer comparisons capture the company’s specific growth outlook, profitability, size and risk profile.

That gap suggests the shares are pricing in somewhat richer expectations than the Fair Ratio implies.

data.title: Microsoft Legal Rift Over OpenAI Cloud Deal Puts Azure AI Story In Focus
data.description:
• Microsoft (NasdaqGS:MSFT) is weighing legal action over OpenAI's reported $50b cloud agreement with Amazon Web Services.
  • The dispute centers on whether that deal conflicts with Microsoft's existing exclusive cloud hosting partnership with OpenAI.
  • This development raises questions about future AI model access, cloud revenue sharing, and large scale hyperscaler alliances.

For investors watching Microsoft, the issue cuts right into the core of its cloud and AI plans. Azure and the close commercial relationship with OpenAI have been central to how the company positions itself in large scale AI infrastructure and services. A challenge to cloud exclusivity touches both on competitive standing and on how Microsoft participates in monetizing leading AI models.

At the same time, hyperscalers are competing hard for AI workloads and long term platform commitments. How this dispute is resolved could influence where developers deploy advanced models, how much traffic flows through Azure versus rival platforms, and what that might mean for Microsoft’s broader role in the AI and cloud markets.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Microsoft by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Microsoft.

Is Microsoft's balance sheet strong enough for future acquisitions? Dive into our detailed financial health analysis.

The potential legal clash over OpenAI’s reported US$50b cloud deal with Amazon goes straight to Microsoft’s contract rights and its position as OpenAI’s primary infrastructure partner. If Microsoft seeks to enforce exclusivity, investors are mainly looking at two angles: whether a court or settlement protects Azure’s share of OpenAI workloads and whether the dispute slows deployment of new AI products that rely on OpenAI models. A prolonged disagreement could introduce uncertainty around how much AI traffic runs on Azure versus Amazon Web Services, which matters for cloud revenue mix and for how Microsoft negotiates future AI model access with other providers like Google Cloud.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Microsoft Narrative

  • This potential lawsuit directly ties into the narrative that Microsoft is building long-duration AI leadership by securing large, contracted workloads on Azure, because enforcing exclusivity would support that catalyst.
  • At the same time, the dispute highlights a risk raised in the narrative around reliance on a small number of large AI customers, since any shift in OpenAI’s deployment plans could affect future usage on Azure.
  • The narrative discusses heavy AI infrastructure spending and customer concentration, but does not fully factor in the possibility of legal remedies, regulatory scrutiny of exclusivity, or contract re-negotiations that might change how AI workloads are shared between hyperscalers.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Microsoft to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Contract disputes with OpenAI and Amazon could introduce uncertainty around future Azure usage, especially if exclusivity terms are reinterpreted or relaxed.
  • ⚠️ Legal proceedings or a public falling out with OpenAI may feed broader concerns about concentration risk in AI customers and Microsoft’s dependence on a few very large AI partnerships.
  • 🎁 A successful enforcement of cloud exclusivity could support Microsoft’s position that high AI capital spending is backed by contractually committed workloads on Azure.
  • 🎁 Even if a compromise is reached, clearer terms on multi-cloud access for OpenAI models could help Microsoft refine how it prices and structures future AI deals with large customers and partners.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, watch for any formal legal filing by Microsoft, public clarification of its contract terms with OpenAI, or updated commentary on Azure’s AI workload mix versus major rivals like Amazon and Alphabet. Earnings calls, regulatory disclosures, and OpenAI’s own product rollout plans for its Frontier platform will help show whether this dispute is contained through negotiations or evolves into a longer legal and commercial reset for Microsoft’s AI and cloud alliances.
